programme.title: Exploring the Long Winter
programme.desc: In this episode, we travel to Jingpo Lake, Xuexiang and Yabuli in Mudanjiang, showcasing the unique winter ice fishing festival, the fairytale-like scenery of Xuexiang, and its world-class ski resorts.

programme.title: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing
programme.desc: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing delves into the historical origins and cultural background of Fengdu Ghost City, and also features the Zhongyuan Festival celebrations and ancestor-worshipping rituals held here. These activities reflect the local people’s positive and optimistic outlook on life and death, as well as the great importance they attach to their ancestors, families and family traditions.

programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: B-girl's power moves
programme.desc: Meet Gao Ya, a 10-year-old girl from Fuqing, Fujian, who goes by the stage name Gao Xing, which means to be happy. She started her break-dancing journey when she was five and has been practicing non-stop ever since. Now as a rising star in her dance squad, she has unlocked many new moves and is ready to take her skill set one step higher.

programme.title: Taohuawu New Year's Painting
programme.desc: Taohuawu New Year woodblock prints, originating from Suzhou, are iconic symbols of Chinese folk art. These vibrant prints, traditionally displayed during Chinese New Year, depict auspicious scenes, deities, and daily life, embodying wishes for prosperity and happiness. Explore the intricate carving and printing techniques that bring these colorful cultural treasures to life.

programme.title: Ding Porcelain Firing
programme.desc:
In ancient times, skilled craftsmen combined clay with fire and, using nothing more than their hands and wisdom, created the history of porcelain.

A thousand years ago, at the time of the Northern Song Dynasty, a type of porcelain was produced that stood out for its exquisite quality, elegant coloring and beautiful patterns. This was Ding porcelain, and it became a favorite at the imperial court. However, within 200 years, amid the chaos of constant warfare, the art of making it was lost. The dazzling white light of the Ding kilns had faded, seemingly forever. 

And yet, 800 years later, that light has been reignited.

programme.title: From Stone to Story
programme.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Crossroads of Cultures: The Legacy of Silk Roads
programme.desc:
Quanzhou, situated on the southeastern coast of China, once served as a maritime hub of the East and Southeast Asia trade network during the 10th to 14th centuries, gaining prominence for its exceptional trading port.
With 22 distinctive heritage sites listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, this city stands as a testament to the legendary history of the Silk Roads.
In this program, we invite four individuals living in Quanzhou to showcase their daily lives, capturing their journeys through the city's world heritage sites.
Through their perspectives, we present a dynamic portrayal of a city rich in heritage. From the maritime splendor of Quanzhou's history to the serene echoes of its ancient city, from the historical treasures of the Luoyang Bridge to the vibrancy of the modern urban landscape, we journey alongside our guests through time and space, exploring Quanzhou's diverse charm and unique allure. Join us as we immerse ourselves in a city that bridges history and cultures, and embraces innovation.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Inner Mongolia’s Frozen Wonderland
programme.desc: In this episode, we feature the Winter Nadam Fair and winter sports in Hulunbuir, Inner Mongolia. By late December, the Hulunbuir Grassland is blanketed in ice and snow. Even in the bitter cold of around 30 degrees below zero, the grassland is alive with a festive bustle.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of Tibetan antelopes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Exploring the Long Winter
programme.desc: In this episode, we travel to Jingpo Lake, Xuexiang and Yabuli in Mudanjiang, showcasing the unique winter ice fishing festival, the fairytale-like scenery of Xuexiang, and its world-class ski resorts.

programme.title: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing
programme.desc: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing delves into the historical origins and cultural background of Fengdu Ghost City, and also features the Zhongyuan Festival celebrations and ancestor-worshipping rituals held here. These activities reflect the local people’s positive and optimistic outlook on life and death, as well as the great importance they attach to their ancestors, families and family traditions.

programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: B-girl's power moves
programme.desc: Meet Gao Ya, a 10-year-old girl from Fuqing, Fujian, who goes by the stage name Gao Xing, which means to be happy. She started her break-dancing journey when she was five and has been practicing non-stop ever since. Now as a rising star in her dance squad, she has unlocked many new moves and is ready to take her skill set one step higher.

programme.title: Taohuawu New Year's Painting
programme.desc: Taohuawu New Year woodblock prints, originating from Suzhou, are iconic symbols of Chinese folk art. These vibrant prints, traditionally displayed during Chinese New Year, depict auspicious scenes, deities, and daily life, embodying wishes for prosperity and happiness. Explore the intricate carving and printing techniques that bring these colorful cultural treasures to life.

programme.title: Ding Porcelain Firing
programme.desc:
In ancient times, skilled craftsmen combined clay with fire and, using nothing more than their hands and wisdom, created the history of porcelain.

A thousand years ago, at the time of the Northern Song Dynasty, a type of porcelain was produced that stood out for its exquisite quality, elegant coloring and beautiful patterns. This was Ding porcelain, and it became a favorite at the imperial court. However, within 200 years, amid the chaos of constant warfare, the art of making it was lost. The dazzling white light of the Ding kilns had faded, seemingly forever. 

And yet, 800 years later, that light has been reignited.

programme.title: From Stone to Story
programme.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Crossroads of Cultures: The Legacy of Silk Roads
programme.desc:
Quanzhou, situated on the southeastern coast of China, once served as a maritime hub of the East and Southeast Asia trade network during the 10th to 14th centuries, gaining prominence for its exceptional trading port.
With 22 distinctive heritage sites listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, this city stands as a testament to the legendary history of the Silk Roads.
In this program, we invite four individuals living in Quanzhou to showcase their daily lives, capturing their journeys through the city's world heritage sites.
Through their perspectives, we present a dynamic portrayal of a city rich in heritage. From the maritime splendor of Quanzhou's history to the serene echoes of its ancient city, from the historical treasures of the Luoyang Bridge to the vibrancy of the modern urban landscape, we journey alongside our guests through time and space, exploring Quanzhou's diverse charm and unique allure. Join us as we immerse ourselves in a city that bridges history and cultures, and embraces innovation.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Inner Mongolia’s Frozen Wonderland
programme.desc: In this episode, we feature the Winter Nadam Fair and winter sports in Hulunbuir, Inner Mongolia. By late December, the Hulunbuir Grassland is blanketed in ice and snow. Even in the bitter cold of around 30 degrees below zero, the grassland is alive with a festive bustle.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of Tibetan antelopes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Exploring the Long Winter
programme.desc: In this episode, we travel to Jingpo Lake, Xuexiang and Yabuli in Mudanjiang, showcasing the unique winter ice fishing festival, the fairytale-like scenery of Xuexiang, and its world-class ski resorts.

programme.title: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing
programme.desc: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing delves into the historical origins and cultural background of Fengdu Ghost City, and also features the Zhongyuan Festival celebrations and ancestor-worshipping rituals held here. These activities reflect the local people’s positive and optimistic outlook on life and death, as well as the great importance they attach to their ancestors, families and family traditions.

programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: B-girl's power moves
programme.desc: Meet Gao Ya, a 10-year-old girl from Fuqing, Fujian, who goes by the stage name Gao Xing, which means to be happy. She started her break-dancing journey when she was five and has been practicing non-stop ever since. Now as a rising star in her dance squad, she has unlocked many new moves and is ready to take her skill set one step higher.

programme.title: Taohuawu New Year's Painting
programme.desc: Taohuawu New Year woodblock prints, originating from Suzhou, are iconic symbols of Chinese folk art. These vibrant prints, traditionally displayed during Chinese New Year, depict auspicious scenes, deities, and daily life, embodying wishes for prosperity and happiness. Explore the intricate carving and printing techniques that bring these colorful cultural treasures to life.

programme.title: Ding Porcelain Firing
programme.desc:
In ancient times, skilled craftsmen combined clay with fire and, using nothing more than their hands and wisdom, created the history of porcelain.

A thousand years ago, at the time of the Northern Song Dynasty, a type of porcelain was produced that stood out for its exquisite quality, elegant coloring and beautiful patterns. This was Ding porcelain, and it became a favorite at the imperial court. However, within 200 years, amid the chaos of constant warfare, the art of making it was lost. The dazzling white light of the Ding kilns had faded, seemingly forever. 

And yet, 800 years later, that light has been reignited.

programme.title: From Stone to Story
programme.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Crossroads of Cultures: The Legacy of Silk Roads
programme.desc:
Quanzhou, situated on the southeastern coast of China, once served as a maritime hub of the East and Southeast Asia trade network during the 10th to 14th centuries, gaining prominence for its exceptional trading port.
With 22 distinctive heritage sites listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, this city stands as a testament to the legendary history of the Silk Roads.
In this program, we invite four individuals living in Quanzhou to showcase their daily lives, capturing their journeys through the city's world heritage sites.
Through their perspectives, we present a dynamic portrayal of a city rich in heritage. From the maritime splendor of Quanzhou's history to the serene echoes of its ancient city, from the historical treasures of the Luoyang Bridge to the vibrancy of the modern urban landscape, we journey alongside our guests through time and space, exploring Quanzhou's diverse charm and unique allure. Join us as we immerse ourselves in a city that bridges history and cultures, and embraces innovation.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Inner Mongolia’s Frozen Wonderland
programme.desc: In this episode, we feature the Winter Nadam Fair and winter sports in Hulunbuir, Inner Mongolia. By late December, the Hulunbuir Grassland is blanketed in ice and snow. Even in the bitter cold of around 30 degrees below zero, the grassland is alive with a festive bustle.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of Tibetan antelopes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Exploring the Long Winter
programme.desc: In this episode, we travel to Jingpo Lake, Xuexiang and Yabuli in Mudanjiang, showcasing the unique winter ice fishing festival, the fairytale-like scenery of Xuexiang, and its world-class ski resorts.

programme.title: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing
programme.desc: Exploring the Folklore of Chongqing delves into the historical origins and cultural background of Fengdu Ghost City, and also features the Zhongyuan Festival celebrations and ancestor-worshipping rituals held here. These activities reflect the local people’s positive and optimistic outlook on life and death, as well as the great importance they attach to their ancestors, families and family traditions.

programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: B-girl's power moves
programme.desc: Meet Gao Ya, a 10-year-old girl from Fuqing, Fujian, who goes by the stage name Gao Xing, which means to be happy. She started her break-dancing journey when she was five and has been practicing non-stop ever since. Now as a rising star in her dance squad, she has unlocked many new moves and is ready to take her skill set one step higher.

programme.title: Taohuawu New Year's Painting
programme.desc: Taohuawu New Year woodblock prints, originating from Suzhou, are iconic symbols of Chinese folk art. These vibrant prints, traditionally displayed during Chinese New Year, depict auspicious scenes, deities, and daily life, embodying wishes for prosperity and happiness. Explore the intricate carving and printing techniques that bring these colorful cultural treasures to life.

programme.title: Ding Porcelain Firing
programme.desc:
In ancient times, skilled craftsmen combined clay with fire and, using nothing more than their hands and wisdom, created the history of porcelain.

A thousand years ago, at the time of the Northern Song Dynasty, a type of porcelain was produced that stood out for its exquisite quality, elegant coloring and beautiful patterns. This was Ding porcelain, and it became a favorite at the imperial court. However, within 200 years, amid the chaos of constant warfare, the art of making it was lost. The dazzling white light of the Ding kilns had faded, seemingly forever. 

And yet, 800 years later, that light has been reignited.

programme.title: From Stone to Story
programme.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS When old craftsmanship meets contemporary art
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Crossroads of Cultures: The Legacy of Silk Roads
programme.desc:
Quanzhou, situated on the southeastern coast of China, once served as a maritime hub of the East and Southeast Asia trade network during the 10th to 14th centuries, gaining prominence for its exceptional trading port.
With 22 distinctive heritage sites listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, this city stands as a testament to the legendary history of the Silk Roads.
In this program, we invite four individuals living in Quanzhou to showcase their daily lives, capturing their journeys through the city's world heritage sites.
Through their perspectives, we present a dynamic portrayal of a city rich in heritage. From the maritime splendor of Quanzhou's history to the serene echoes of its ancient city, from the historical treasures of the Luoyang Bridge to the vibrancy of the modern urban landscape, we journey alongside our guests through time and space, exploring Quanzhou's diverse charm and unique allure. Join us as we immerse ourselves in a city that bridges history and cultures, and embraces innovation.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Life as a Miao Silversmith
programme.desc: In this episode, Eva May steps into Maliao Village — a century-old silversmith village nestled in Leishan, Guizhou Province. Over the course of a week, she dives into the craft and daily lives of Miao men who carry on the silversmithing legacy. She works side by side with local silversmiths day in and day out, delving into the culture and techniques behind Miao silverware. She even joins master silversmiths for a live-stream event, before engaging in discussions with university professors about how traditional craftsmanship can fuel rural revitalization. On top of that, she witnesses the breathtaking sight of \"maidens adorned in silver and flowers\" at the grand Miao New Year celebrations, visits Xijiang Qianhu Miaozhai — the world’s largest Miao village — and learns to forge a small yet meaningful piece of Miao silver ornament.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of golden snub-nosed monkeys
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: New Year with the Miao
programme.desc: In this episode, it’s Miao New Year, and that means multitudes of merrymaking! Fishing, drinking, bullfighting… Join the host Min-Zhui Lee, among the magnificent mountains of Leishan, where it’s all happening for the ethnic Miao.

programme.title: Valentine’s for the Pumi People
programme.desc: In this episode, we record the grand celebration of the traditional Pumi Valentine's Day in Luoguqing, Lanping County, Yunnan Province. Every year, on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, young men and women from the local community gather on the vast meadows of Luoguqing to take part in singing and dancing activities in pursuit of marriage. During the program, the host interacts with local people of the Pumi and other ethnic groups, showcasing the traditional plateau lifestyle of the Pumi people, their distinctive firepit culture, as well as the community’s brand-new look in the new era. All these scenes unfold against the lively backdrop of the Valentine’s Day activities, fostering a warm atmosphere of ethnic solidarity.

programme.title: Valentine's with the Snake King
programme.desc: Every year on Qixi Festival (Chinese Valentine's Day), the town of Zhanghu in Nanping, Fujian Province hosts a folk custom event: the Snake King Parade. Local residents carry the statue of the Snake King from the temple, along with specially prepared pythons, and parade through the streets and alleys. Wherever the procession passes, residents and shopkeepers greet them with firecrackers and burn incense to pray, hoping to receive the unique blessing of the Snake King. This distinctive folk activity is rarely seen elsewhere across China. Taking this event as its highlight, the episode leads audiences on a deep dive into the towns and villages of northern Fujian, delivering a highly engaging and visually appealing viewing experience.

programme.title: Born to climb
programme.desc: Bobby, aka Lin Ziguan, is a passionate young climber in Wenzhou City, east China's Zhejiang Province. He first came across the sport when his parents took him to an indoor climbing facility at a local shopping mall for fun, and it was a case of love at first sight. To further fuel his passion, Bobby's father refurnished a room at home and turned it into a private rock-climbing gym. While many students find themselves \"climbing the walls\" with frustration on weeknights and grumbling at their homework, Bobby spends most of his free time clambering around the surfaces of his bedroom as he aspires to become a celebrated outdoor rock climber one day.

programme.title: Kesi
programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe
programme.desc: On the occasion of the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations between China and the EU, Inheritors of China launches a special multimedia series titled “Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe.” Taking culture as the starting point, the series responds to public discourse by telling the real-life stories of Europeans living in China. Through their perspectives, it vividly illustrates how tradition and modernity spark mutual inspiration between China and Europe—showcasing a relationship of shared growth, empowerment, and enduring partnership.\nThe series travels through cities including Beijing, Shanghai, Jinan, and Datong, focusing on Europeans of different ages and professions—from programming and installation art to music, calligraphy, and martial arts. Their lived experiences offer fresh insight into how cultural exchange between China and Europe continues to gain vitality, and how mutual learning between civilizations breathes lasting life into practical cooperation.

programme.title: The Sichuan Zhuqin
programme.desc: Sichuan Zhuqin is a national intangible cultural heritage in China. Nowadays, its performances are constantly innovated, with new styles such as ensemble emerging. Creators have broken traditional performance methods, integrating Zhuqin with traditional Chinese instruments like the guzheng and erhu, and also experimenting with contemporary music forms such as rap and folk rock, aiming to make this traditional art appeal to more young audiences.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS “Tribute tea” to more tea lovers
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Tales in Light and Shadow
programme.desc: Beijing shadow puppets bring ancient Chinese tales to life with intricately crafted leather figures. Behind an illuminated screen, these colorful silhouettes dance, enacting epic stories and folklore accompanied by traditional music. Discover this captivating performing art, a cherished part of China's rich cultural tapestry and a mesmerizing visual spectacle.

programme.title: Life as a Miao Silversmith
programme.desc: In this episode, Eva May steps into Maliao Village — a century-old silversmith village nestled in Leishan, Guizhou Province. Over the course of a week, she dives into the craft and daily lives of Miao men who carry on the silversmithing legacy. She works side by side with local silversmiths day in and day out, delving into the culture and techniques behind Miao silverware. She even joins master silversmiths for a live-stream event, before engaging in discussions with university professors about how traditional craftsmanship can fuel rural revitalization. On top of that, she witnesses the breathtaking sight of \"maidens adorned in silver and flowers\" at the grand Miao New Year celebrations, visits Xijiang Qianhu Miaozhai — the world’s largest Miao village — and learns to forge a small yet meaningful piece of Miao silver ornament.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of golden snub-nosed monkeys
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: New Year with the Miao
programme.desc: In this episode, it’s Miao New Year, and that means multitudes of merrymaking! Fishing, drinking, bullfighting… Join the host Min-Zhui Lee, among the magnificent mountains of Leishan, where it’s all happening for the ethnic Miao.

programme.title: Valentine’s for the Pumi People
programme.desc: In this episode, we record the grand celebration of the traditional Pumi Valentine's Day in Luoguqing, Lanping County, Yunnan Province. Every year, on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, young men and women from the local community gather on the vast meadows of Luoguqing to take part in singing and dancing activities in pursuit of marriage. During the program, the host interacts with local people of the Pumi and other ethnic groups, showcasing the traditional plateau lifestyle of the Pumi people, their distinctive firepit culture, as well as the community’s brand-new look in the new era. All these scenes unfold against the lively backdrop of the Valentine’s Day activities, fostering a warm atmosphere of ethnic solidarity.

programme.title: Valentine's with the Snake King
programme.desc: Every year on Qixi Festival (Chinese Valentine's Day), the town of Zhanghu in Nanping, Fujian Province hosts a folk custom event: the Snake King Parade. Local residents carry the statue of the Snake King from the temple, along with specially prepared pythons, and parade through the streets and alleys. Wherever the procession passes, residents and shopkeepers greet them with firecrackers and burn incense to pray, hoping to receive the unique blessing of the Snake King. This distinctive folk activity is rarely seen elsewhere across China. Taking this event as its highlight, the episode leads audiences on a deep dive into the towns and villages of northern Fujian, delivering a highly engaging and visually appealing viewing experience.

programme.title: Born to climb
programme.desc: Bobby, aka Lin Ziguan, is a passionate young climber in Wenzhou City, east China's Zhejiang Province. He first came across the sport when his parents took him to an indoor climbing facility at a local shopping mall for fun, and it was a case of love at first sight. To further fuel his passion, Bobby's father refurnished a room at home and turned it into a private rock-climbing gym. While many students find themselves \"climbing the walls\" with frustration on weeknights and grumbling at their homework, Bobby spends most of his free time clambering around the surfaces of his bedroom as he aspires to become a celebrated outdoor rock climber one day.

programme.title: Kesi
programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe
programme.desc: On the occasion of the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations between China and the EU, Inheritors of China launches a special multimedia series titled “Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe.” Taking culture as the starting point, the series responds to public discourse by telling the real-life stories of Europeans living in China. Through their perspectives, it vividly illustrates how tradition and modernity spark mutual inspiration between China and Europe—showcasing a relationship of shared growth, empowerment, and enduring partnership.\nThe series travels through cities including Beijing, Shanghai, Jinan, and Datong, focusing on Europeans of different ages and professions—from programming and installation art to music, calligraphy, and martial arts. Their lived experiences offer fresh insight into how cultural exchange between China and Europe continues to gain vitality, and how mutual learning between civilizations breathes lasting life into practical cooperation.

programme.title: The Sichuan Zhuqin
programme.desc: Sichuan Zhuqin is a national intangible cultural heritage in China. Nowadays, its performances are constantly innovated, with new styles such as ensemble emerging. Creators have broken traditional performance methods, integrating Zhuqin with traditional Chinese instruments like the guzheng and erhu, and also experimenting with contemporary music forms such as rap and folk rock, aiming to make this traditional art appeal to more young audiences.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS “Tribute tea” to more tea lovers
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Tales in Light and Shadow
programme.desc: Beijing shadow puppets bring ancient Chinese tales to life with intricately crafted leather figures. Behind an illuminated screen, these colorful silhouettes dance, enacting epic stories and folklore accompanied by traditional music. Discover this captivating performing art, a cherished part of China's rich cultural tapestry and a mesmerizing visual spectacle.

programme.title: Life as a Miao Silversmith
programme.desc: In this episode, Eva May steps into Maliao Village — a century-old silversmith village nestled in Leishan, Guizhou Province. Over the course of a week, she dives into the craft and daily lives of Miao men who carry on the silversmithing legacy. She works side by side with local silversmiths day in and day out, delving into the culture and techniques behind Miao silverware. She even joins master silversmiths for a live-stream event, before engaging in discussions with university professors about how traditional craftsmanship can fuel rural revitalization. On top of that, she witnesses the breathtaking sight of \"maidens adorned in silver and flowers\" at the grand Miao New Year celebrations, visits Xijiang Qianhu Miaozhai — the world’s largest Miao village — and learns to forge a small yet meaningful piece of Miao silver ornament.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of golden snub-nosed monkeys
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: New Year with the Miao
programme.desc: In this episode, it’s Miao New Year, and that means multitudes of merrymaking! Fishing, drinking, bullfighting… Join the host Min-Zhui Lee, among the magnificent mountains of Leishan, where it’s all happening for the ethnic Miao.

programme.title: Valentine’s for the Pumi People
programme.desc: In this episode, we record the grand celebration of the traditional Pumi Valentine's Day in Luoguqing, Lanping County, Yunnan Province. Every year, on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, young men and women from the local community gather on the vast meadows of Luoguqing to take part in singing and dancing activities in pursuit of marriage. During the program, the host interacts with local people of the Pumi and other ethnic groups, showcasing the traditional plateau lifestyle of the Pumi people, their distinctive firepit culture, as well as the community’s brand-new look in the new era. All these scenes unfold against the lively backdrop of the Valentine’s Day activities, fostering a warm atmosphere of ethnic solidarity.

programme.title: Valentine's with the Snake King
programme.desc: Every year on Qixi Festival (Chinese Valentine's Day), the town of Zhanghu in Nanping, Fujian Province hosts a folk custom event: the Snake King Parade. Local residents carry the statue of the Snake King from the temple, along with specially prepared pythons, and parade through the streets and alleys. Wherever the procession passes, residents and shopkeepers greet them with firecrackers and burn incense to pray, hoping to receive the unique blessing of the Snake King. This distinctive folk activity is rarely seen elsewhere across China. Taking this event as its highlight, the episode leads audiences on a deep dive into the towns and villages of northern Fujian, delivering a highly engaging and visually appealing viewing experience.

programme.title: Born to climb
programme.desc: Bobby, aka Lin Ziguan, is a passionate young climber in Wenzhou City, east China's Zhejiang Province. He first came across the sport when his parents took him to an indoor climbing facility at a local shopping mall for fun, and it was a case of love at first sight. To further fuel his passion, Bobby's father refurnished a room at home and turned it into a private rock-climbing gym. While many students find themselves \"climbing the walls\" with frustration on weeknights and grumbling at their homework, Bobby spends most of his free time clambering around the surfaces of his bedroom as he aspires to become a celebrated outdoor rock climber one day.

programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe
programme.desc: On the occasion of the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations between China and the EU, Inheritors of China launches a special multimedia series titled “Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe.” Taking culture as the starting point, the series responds to public discourse by telling the real-life stories of Europeans living in China. Through their perspectives, it vividly illustrates how tradition and modernity spark mutual inspiration between China and Europe—showcasing a relationship of shared growth, empowerment, and enduring partnership.\nThe series travels through cities including Beijing, Shanghai, Jinan, and Datong, focusing on Europeans of different ages and professions—from programming and installation art to music, calligraphy, and martial arts. Their lived experiences offer fresh insight into how cultural exchange between China and Europe continues to gain vitality, and how mutual learning between civilizations breathes lasting life into practical cooperation.

programme.desc: Sichuan Zhuqin is a national intangible cultural heritage in China. Nowadays, its performances are constantly innovated, with new styles such as ensemble emerging. Creators have broken traditional performance methods, integrating Zhuqin with traditional Chinese instruments like the guzheng and erhu, and also experimenting with contemporary music forms such as rap and folk rock, aiming to make this traditional art appeal to more young audiences.

They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

During the program, the host interacts with local people of the Pumi and other ethnic groups, showcasing the traditional plateau lifestyle of the Pumi people, their distinctive firepit culture, as well as the community’s brand-new look in the new era. All these scenes unfold against the lively backdrop of the Valentine’s Day activities, fostering a warm atmosphere of ethnic solidarity.

Wherever the procession passes, residents and shopkeepers greet them with firecrackers and burn incense to pray, hoping to receive the unique blessing of the Snake King. This distinctive folk activity is rarely seen elsewhere across China. Taking this event as its highlight, the episode leads audiences on a deep dive into the towns and villages of northern Fujian, delivering a highly engaging and visually appealing viewing experience.

To further fuel his passion, Bobby's father refurnished a room at home and turned it into a private rock-climbing gym. While many students find themselves \"climbing the walls\" with frustration on weeknights and grumbling at their homework, Bobby spends most of his free time clambering around the surfaces of his bedroom as he aspires to become a celebrated outdoor rock climber one day.

programme.title: Kesi
programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe
programme.desc: On the occasion of the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations between China and the EU, Inheritors of China launches a special multimedia series titled “Dream Builders: Stories from China and Europe.” Taking culture as the starting point, the series responds to public discourse by telling the real-life stories of Europeans living in China. Through their perspectives, it vividly illustrates how tradition and modernity spark mutual inspiration between China and Europe—showcasing a relationship of shared growth, empowerment, and enduring partnership.\nThe series travels through cities including Beijing, Shanghai, Jinan, and Datong, focusing on Europeans of different ages and professions—from programming and installation art to music, calligraphy, and martial arts. Their lived experiences offer fresh insight into how cultural exchange between China and Europe continues to gain vitality, and how mutual learning between civilizations breathes lasting life into practical cooperation.

programme.title: The Sichuan Zhuqin
programme.desc: Sichuan Zhuqin is a national intangible cultural heritage in China. Nowadays, its performances are constantly innovated, with new styles such as ensemble emerging. Creators have broken traditional performance methods, integrating Zhuqin with traditional Chinese instruments like the guzheng and erhu, and also experimenting with contemporary music forms such as rap and folk rock, aiming to make this traditional art appeal to more young audiences.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS “Tribute tea” to more tea lovers
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Living Arts of Huizhou
programme.desc: This is Huangshan in eastern China. In this city, you’ll encounter one of China’s most traditional building styles: Hui-style architecture. How does Hui style set this region apart? What’s life like for people living in these houses? With these questions in mind, we’re embarking on a journey to decode Hui-style architecture.

programme.title: Tales in Light and Shadow
programme.desc: Beijing shadow puppets bring ancient Chinese tales to life with intricately crafted leather figures. Behind an illuminated screen, these colorful silhouettes dance, enacting epic stories and folklore accompanied by traditional music. Discover this captivating performing art, a cherished part of China's rich cultural tapestry and a mesmerizing visual spectacle.

programme.title: The Pulse
programme.desc: “The Pulse” features musicians performing amidst Guizhou’s stunning natural scenery, bringing to life a harmonious blend of music and landscape.

programme.title: The Pulse
programme.desc: “The Pulse” features musicians performing amidst Guizhou’s stunning natural scenery, bringing to life a harmonious blend of music and landscape.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of black-necked cranes
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: The Great Wall Homestay
programme.desc: In this episode of Travelogue, Tianran He's heading to the mountains on Beijing’s northern outskirts, to meet some people his age who have swapped city life for the chance to run guesthouses beside the Great Wall of China.

programme.title: A Village Homestay in Beijing
programme.desc: In this episode of Travelogue, our presenter Tianran He’s heading out of Beijing proper, to the city’s Miyun District. There he’ll meet some people running guesthouses who gave up their day jobs, in order to achieve the dream of a place of their own in the country.
